english: Lt and S.g.? p´rum-rús (phrum rus) cartilage, gristle.

, ˚p´red ('phred), cross, transverse; across, athwart, obliquely; p´réd-du (phred du), col. *t´ed-t´ed-la* (*thred thred la*), crossways, in a cross direction; p´réd-lam (phred lam), a path (horizontal or inclined) leading along the side of a mountain, (cf. on the other hand ˚p´raṅ ('phrang)); p´red-gtán (phred gtan) bolt or bar of a gate; *t´éd-la dáṅ-po* (*thred la dang po*), horizontal W.
something like: a child's frock or chemise Ld. (?)
etc. v. ˚p´rog ('phrog); ཕྲོབ་, ཕྲོལ་ p´rob (phrob), p´rol (phrol) v. ˚p´rob ('phrob), ˚p´rol ('phrol).

, pf. ˚p´ags ('phags), 1. to rise, to be raised, e.g. a post or stake raised by the frost; to soar up, to fly up to heaven, a miraculous feat often performed by the saints of legends, Dzl. and elsewh.; of rays of light, Dzl. and elsewh.; fig.: to be higher, more elevated, deï stéṅ-du (de'i steng du) (or dé-las (de las)) dpag-tsád brgyad-k´ri (dpag tshad brgyad khri) (or more accurately k´ris (khris)) ˚p´ágs-so ('phags so) Glr., Pth., (this region) lies by 80000 miles higher than that Stg.; to grow larger, longer, of the apparent lengthening of the teeth when aching W.; of horses: to rear, to rise up on the hind-legs; more particularly of the deifying of saints; thus the demi-god-like king Srontsansgampo in his farewell speech says: kyed kun ˚p´ágs-pai byin-rlabs yin (khyed kun 'phags pa'i byin rlabs yin) I am the divine instrument of your elevation (your elevation-blessing), he who will effect your ascent to heaven or deification; part. pf. ˚p´ágs-pa ('phags pa) (Ssk. आर्य), sublime, exalted, raised above, p´ál-las ˚p´ágs-par bzáṅ-ba (phal las 'phags par bzang ba) a more than ordinary beauty Dzl.; gżan-pas ˚p´ágs-par gyúr-to (gzhan pas 'phags par gyur to) he far excelled others Dzl.; kyád-(par) ˚p´ags-(pa) (khyad (par) 'phags (pa)), distinguished, excellent, glorious, yúl-las kyad-˚p´ags rgya-gar-yul (yul las khyad 'phags rgya gar yul) India, the most glorious country; nór-sna kyád-par ˚p´ágs-pa brgyai búl-ba (nor sna khyad par 'phags pa brgya'i bul ba) an offering of a hundred of the most costly kinds of jewels Pth.; esp. in reference to holy persons, things, places etc.; title of saints, and teachers of religion, with the fem. ˚p´ágs-ma ('phags ma); ˚p´ágs-pa ('phags pa) 'par excellence' is Avalokitesvara, in W. esp. the one, that has his throne at Triloknath in Chamba, v. re-˚p´ágs (re 'phags); the word is also frq. used as an epithet, placed at the head of the title-pages of religious writings; lastly it is a name of common persons. — ˚p´ágs-pai nor bdun ('phags pa'i nor bdun) the seven treasures of the saints: sbyín-pa (sbyin pa), tsúl-k´rims (tshul khrims), dád-pa (dad pa) and the like Mil. — ˚p´ágs(-pai) yul ('phags (pa'i) yul) 1. elevated country, highland. 2. the holy land of the Buddhists, the tracts of the middle Ganges; ˚p´ags-pai skad ('phags pa'i skad), the Sanskrit language Lex. — ˚p´ags-rgyal ('phags rgyal) Tar. and elsewh. = उज्जयिनी Schf., town and district of Ujain. — 2. the word is stated to imply also to play, to joke, to make sport C.

1. v. p´aṅ (phang) I. — 2. also ˚p´aṅs ('phangs), dpaṅs (dpangs), spaṅs (spangs), height, ˚p´áṅ-du ('phang du), ˚p´áṅs-su ('phangs su) in height; k´ri-˚p´aṅ (khri 'phang) v. k´ri (khri), go-˚p´aṅ (go 'phang) v. go (go); gnam-˚p´aṅ (gnam 'phang), the height of the heavens Lex., Mil.; dbu-˚p´aṅ (dbu 'phang) fig. highness, sublimity, dkon-mčóg-gi dbu-˚p´aṅ smád-pa (dkon mchog gi dbu 'phang smad pa) to lower, to detract from the sublimity of God (v. dkon-mčóg (dkon mchog)), to blaspheme God Doman; ˚p´aṅs-mtó ('phangs mtho) high Dzl.; ˚p´aṅs-mto-ba ('phangs mtho ba), ˚p´aṅs-mton-dmán ('phangs mthon dman) relative height Dzl.

1. frq. for p´aṅs-pa (phangs pa) to spare, to save Dzl.; kindly and carefully to protect from harm, e.g. a drunken Lama Thgy.; hence prob. the version प्रेयंस; ˚p´aṅs - méd gtóṅ-sems-ldan ('phangs med gtong sems ldan) liberal, bounteous, without restriction S.g. — 2. Glr. also for ˚p´óṅs-pa ('phongs pa) provinc.

I. v. p´an (phan) (I). — II. ˚p´aṅ-yul ('phang yul) Glr., ˚p´án-po ('phan po) Huc II, 242; name of the nearest alpine valley north of Lhasa, the inhabitants of which are said to speak an indistinct dialect.
